title: Momentum & Impulse
subject: physics
summary: Momentum describes motion through mass and velocity, and it is central to collision and explosion problems.
SPTG commonly tests direct momentum calculation, impulse, and conservation in one-dimensional collisions.
Harder problems include sign conventions, recoil, elastic versus inelastic behavior, and linking force-time graphs to impulse.
coreConcepts: Momentum is a vector quantity: p = mv.
A heavier object or a faster object has greater momentum.
If external net force is negligible, total momentum is conserved.
In collisions, momentum is always conserved for an isolated system.
Kinetic energy is not always conserved, but momentum is.
In a perfectly inelastic collision, objects stick together after impact.
In explosions, total momentum before and after remains the same if no external impulse acts.
Direction matters, so signs must be assigned carefully in one-dimensional problems.
The area under a force-time graph equals impulse.
derive: Momentum is defined as mass times velocity, so p = mv.
Newton's second law in momentum form is F = dp/dt.
Multiplying both sides by time interval gives Ft = Δp.
This leads to impulse J = FΔt = Δp for constant force.
For a system of bodies with negligible external force, internal forces cancel in pairs, so total momentum remains constant.
Thus total momentum before collision equals total momentum after collision.
formulas: Impulse-momentum theorem: J = Δp = p_final - p_initial
Conservation of momentum: total momentum before = total momentum after
Perfectly inelastic collision: m1u1 + m2u2 = (m1 + m2)v
Force from momentum change: F = Δp / Δt
traps: Momentum is a vector, so direction signs matter.
Do not assume kinetic energy is conserved unless the problem states elastic behavior or it is implied.
In stick-together collisions, final mass becomes the sum of masses.
A large force over short time can produce the same impulse as a small force over long time.
Explosion problems often start from total momentum zero.
Do not mix up impulse with force; impulse includes time.
When using conservation, check whether external force can be neglected.

sptgSet.question: Find the momentum of a 2 kg object moving at 5 m/s.
A 4 kg object is at rest. Find its momentum.
A force of 10 N acts for 3 s on an object. Find the impulse.
A 3 kg object changes velocity from 2 m/s to 6 m/s. Find the change in momentum.
A 5 kg cart moving at 4 m/s collides with a wall and rebounds at 2 m/s in the opposite direction. Find the change in momentum.
sptgSet.answer: -30 kg·m/s if initial direction is positive

sptgSet.question: A 2 kg object initially at rest experiences an impulse of 14 N·s. Find its final velocity.
Two objects of masses 2 kg and 3 kg move at 4 m/s and 0 m/s respectively. They stick together. Find their final velocity.
A 1 kg ball moving at 6 m/s hits a 2 kg stationary ball and they move together. Find their common velocity.
A force-time graph is a rectangle of height 8 N and width 5 s. Find the impulse.
A gun of mass 4 kg fires a bullet of mass 0.02 kg at 200 m/s. Find the recoil speed of the gun.

hardSet.question: A 2 kg object moving at 10 m/s collides head-on with a 3 kg object moving at 4 m/s in the opposite direction. They stick together. Find the final velocity.
hardSet.answer: 2 m/s in the 2 kg object's original direction

hardSet.question: A 0.5 kg ball moving at 8 m/s rebounds at 6 m/s in the opposite direction after hitting a wall. Find the impulse on the ball.
hardSet.answer: -7 N·s if initial direction is positive

hardSet.question: A 1200 kg car moving at 20 m/s collides with a 800 kg car moving at rest. If they stick together, find the common velocity.
A 3 kg object initially moves at 5 m/s. A constant force acts for 4 s and changes its velocity to 9 m/s. Find the force.
A 1 kg ball drops vertically and hits the ground at 10 m/s, then rebounds upward at 6 m/s. Find the change in momentum.
hardSet.answer: 16 kg·m/s upward if upward is positive

hardSet.question: A 2 kg trolley moving at 3 m/s receives an impulse of 10 N·s in the same direction. Find its new speed.
Two ice skaters of masses 50 kg and 70 kg push off from rest. If the 50 kg skater moves backward at 4 m/s, find the speed of the 70 kg skater.
A 0.1 kg ball moving at 30 m/s is struck and leaves in the opposite direction at 20 m/s. If contact time is 0.01 s, find average force.
A 4 kg cart moving at 6 m/s catches a 1 kg bag dropped vertically into it. Find the new horizontal speed of the cart-bag system.
A force-time graph is triangular with base 4 s and height 12 N. Find impulse.

pat3Set.question: A 2 kg object moving at 8 m/s collides elastically with a 2 kg object at rest. Find the final velocities.
pat3Set.answer: First stops, second moves at 8 m/s

pat3Set.question: A 3 kg object moving at 10 m/s collides elastically head-on with a 1 kg object at rest. Find the final velocities.
A shell initially at rest explodes into two fragments of masses 2 kg and 3 kg. If the 2 kg fragment moves at 12 m/s, find the velocity of the 3 kg fragment.
A 0.2 kg ball moving at 25 m/s is brought to rest in 0.05 s. Find average force.
A force-time graph consists of a rectangle 6 N by 3 s followed by a triangle of base 2 s and height 6 N. Find total impulse.
A 5 kg block moving at 4 m/s collides with a 3 kg block moving at -2 m/s. If the final velocity of the 5 kg block is 1 m/s, find the final velocity of the 3 kg block.
A 1 kg object receives a variable force such that the force-time graph is a straight line from 0 N at t=0 to 10 N at t=4 s. If initially at rest, find final velocity.
A rocket ejects gas backward. State which conservation law explains its motion and why.
pat3Set.answer: Conservation of momentum; forward rocket momentum balances backward gas momentum

pat3Set.question: A 10 g bullet moving at 300 m/s embeds in a 1.49 kg wooden block initially at rest. Find the speed just after impact.
A 2 kg body moving at 5 m/s is acted on by a net force that changes linearly from 0 to 8 N over 3 s. Find final momentum.
